notes.note: Varro, to whom Cicero has now reassigned the Academica, recasting the dialogue's speakers. He rebuilt the work from two books into four for the new dedication.
The aristocratic interlocutors Catulus, Lucullus, and Hortensius of the first edition of the Academica, whom Cicero is removing. He thought such grandees ill-suited to talk technical philosophy.

notes.anchor: converted it from two books into four
notes.note: Cicero is describing the second edition of his Academica (the so-called Academica Posteriora), expanded from a two-book to a four-book form with Varro as a speaker. Only fragments of this version survive.
Marcus Brutus, dedicatee of the companion De Finibus; Cicero wonders, half in jest, whether Varro's jealousy is directed at him. "That, by Hercules, was all we needed," he adds in mock dismay.

notes.anchor: a sense of shame before the Trojans
notes.note: A Homeric tag (Hector's words, Iliad 6.442): the dread of what people will say. Cicero fears that whatever he writes for Dolabella will draw public criticism.
Publius Cornelius Dolabella, Cicero's ex-son-in-law and a Caesarian, who kept pressing Cicero for some literary or political composition. Cicero cannot think what to give him.
Atticus's young daughter Attica, whose continuing illness tormented Cicero. He keeps re-reading Atticus's letters for news of her.
A freedman of the deceased Brinnius who, by Roman custom, shared in his former patron's estate alongside Cicero as a co-heir. Cicero is a reluctant party to this minor inheritance.
Albius Sabinus, another co-heir to the Brinnian estate. He and the freedman wished to confer with Cicero in person, which Cicero declines as not worth the trouble.

notes.anchor: it is the third before the Ides
notes.note: The auction date, reckoned in the Roman backward style from the Ides (the 13th of this month); the third day before, counted inclusively, is 11 July. The Ides fell on the 13th in months other than March, May, July, and October.
